SiO2.name: Silica
SiO2.notes: Primary glass former. High amounts increase melting temperature and thermal durability. Source: quartz, flint, silica.

Al2O3.name: Alumina
Al2O3.notes: Stabilizes the glass structure, prevents running. High Al2O3 promotes matte surfaces. Source: kaolin, ball clay, feldspar.

K2O.name: Potassium Oxide
K2O.notes: High thermal expansion. Produces bright, glossy surfaces. Source: potash feldspar (Custer, G-200).

Na2O.name: Sodium Oxide
Na2O.notes: Very high thermal expansion. Active flux at low temperatures. Source: soda feldspar, nepheline syenite, soda ash.

CaO.name: Calcium Oxide
CaO.notes: Moderate thermal expansion. Promotes hard, durable glazes. Promotes celadon and turquoise colors. Source: whiting, wollastonite, dolomite.

MgO.name: Magnesium Oxide
MgO.notes: Very low thermal expansion. Promotes matte surfaces. With cobalt, shifts toward lavender. Source: dolomite, talc, magnesium carbonate.

Fe2O3.name: Iron(III) Oxide
Fe2O3.notes: Most versatile colorant. Oxidation: amber/tan. Reduction: celadon green, tenmoku black. Also a flux at high percentages.

FeO.name: Iron(II) Oxide
FeO.notes: Formed during reduction firing from Fe2O3. Contributes to darker, iron-rich reduction colors.

CuO.name: Copper(II) Oxide
CuO.notes: Oxidation: green/turquoise. Reduction: copper red, metallic. Ca-fluxed = turquoise; Na-fluxed = blue-green. Also a flux.

CoO.name: Cobalt(II) Oxide
CoO.notes: Most reliable colorant. Strong blue in any atmosphere. With Mg = lavender. With Fe = blue-green. Very strong; >2% tends black.

MnO.name: Manganese(II) Oxide
MnO.notes: Oxidation: purple (low %) to brown (high %). Reduction: metallic brown/black. VOLATILE above 1900F — toxic fumes. Always ventilate.

TiO2.name: Titanium Dioxide
TiO2.notes: Opacifier and crystal promoter. In rutile form, creates subtle blue/tan variegation. Can promote mottled effects.

ZnO.name: Zinc Oxide
ZnO.notes: Flux and opacifier. LOW thermal expansion. CRITICAL: turns chrome-tin pinks brown. Use zinc-free bases for pinks. Promotes crystal growth.

BaO.name: Barium Oxide
BaO.notes: Used in matte glazes. Soluble barium is toxic — avoid in functional ware. Barium carbonate is relatively insoluble but can leach under acidic conditions.

SrO.name: Strontium Oxide
SrO.notes: Similar to CaO but slightly lower melting point. Sometimes used as a safer substitute for barium in matte glazes.

Li2O.name: Lithium Oxide
Li2O.notes: Most powerful flux. Very low thermal expansion. Used in small amounts to lower melting temperature. Source: lithium carbonate, spodumene.

B2O3.name: Boron Oxide
B2O3.notes: Very powerful flux from borax, gerstley borate, frits. Lowers melting temperature significantly. Can turn blue glazes green. Common in low-fire and mid-range glazes.

SnO2.name: Tin Oxide
SnO2.notes: Expensive but effective opacifier. With chrome = pink. With copper = pink-red. Low toxicity alternative to zirconium.

ZrO2.name: Zirconium Oxide
ZrO2.notes: Primary opacifier in commercial glazes. Cheaper than tin oxide. Source: zircopax, zirconium silicate. Very stable and inert.

Cr2O3.name: Chromium(III) Oxide
Cr2O3.notes: Strong green. With tin = pink. With zinc = brown (unwanted). Can cause crawling/pinholing in some bases. In reduction, may darken and mute.

P2O5.name: Phosphorus Pentoxide
P2O5.notes: Found in bone ash. Produces milky/opalescent effects. Can promote micro-crystal formation.
